content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Every Friday I’d open Claude Code, point it at our Sentry, and type some version of go through the unresolved issues and fix what you can. Then I’d wander off to standup.
Forty minutes later there’d be three, sometimes four PRs sitting there waiting. Half of them genuinely good. One time it found a null check I’d been meaning to add since March and just… added it.
I was proud of that little setup. Told two coworkers about it. One of them copied it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Then I read the Tenet Security writeup on Tuesday and felt my stomach do a slow roll.
What they found, and I’ll keep it plain because the plain version is the scary one: your Sentry DSN, the key your frontend uses to send errors, is public. It has to be.
It’s sitting in your client-side JavaScript right now. Anyone who opens devtools on your site can read it in about eight seconds. I know the number because I timed myself doing it to my own app, third coffee going cold next to me, muttering no, surely it’s not just sitting there. It was just sitting there.
That DSN lets you POST error events into someone’s Sentry. By design. That’s the entire product, catching errors from the wild, so there’s no real auth on the ingest endpoint beyond the key that’s already public.
So an attacker writes a fake error. Not a crash. A message. They format it as markdown that reads exactly like a Sentry here’s how to resolve this note, a tidy little resolution step that says run this command, install this package, npx @some-validation-thing. And they POST it to your Sentry like it's a real bug from a real user.
Now the part that makes it land. When your coding agent pulls unresolved Sentry issues through the Sentry MCP server, it doesn’t get a flag saying by the way, a real human application threw this one.
It just gets text. Trusted text, as far as the agent’s concerned, because it came back from a tool the agent is wired to believe.
The model genuinely cannot tell the difference between an error your app coughed up at 2am and one some stranger typed into your error tracker from a coffee shop in another country.
And the agent has hands. It can run npx, install packages, touch the shell. So it runs the thing.

The number that stuck
Tenet’s researchers (Ron Bobrov, Barak Sternberg, Nevo Poran) named it Agent jacking, which, sure, every attack needs a name now. The figure I can’t put down is 85%.
Eighty-five percent success rate across the agents they threw it at. Claude Code, Cursor, Codex. The exact tools half of us live in all day.
They turned up 2,388 organizations with DSNs you could inject into. Seventy-one of those in the Tranco top million.
The range ran from a roughly $250-billion company down to people running side projects on a hand-me-down laptop, and somewhere in the middle of that list, a cloud-security vendor, which I find funny in the specific way that things are funny right before they stop being funny.
More than a hundred times, in their controlled test, an agent actually picked up the poisoned error and ran the payload.
And what does the payload reach for, once it’s running on your box? The usual yard sale. Environment variables, AWS keys, GitHub tokens, the kube token, npm creds, your git remote URLs, VPN access.
Everything a dev machine has lying around, because the dev machine is exactly where you keep the keys to everything so you don’t have to type them forty times a day.
There’s a line from the writeup I keep rereading: the attacker never touches the victim’s infrastructure.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The malicious instruction arrives disguised as a legitimate ‘Resolution’ inside an ordinary error. They didn’t break into anything. They filed a bug report. You — well, your agent, which is sort of you now — did the rest.

I kept waiting for the patch line
I expected the tidy ending. The Sentry shipped a fix, bump your version line that lets you close the tab and feel fine. That’s not what happened. Sentry’s response, more or less, was that this is technically not defensible. They declined to fix it at the root.
They switched on a content filter that blocks one specific payload string, which is the security equivalent of learning a single knock and refusing to open the door for that one knock while leaving it unlocked.
And honestly, they’re not wrong that it isn’t really theirs to fix. Their whole job is to accept arbitrary junk from the open internet, because errors are arbitrary junk from the open internet.
The bug isn’t in Sentry. The bug is the assumption every one of us made the day we plugged an MCP server into an agent that can run commands: that tool output is data, and the agent will treat it like data.
It won’t. To the model it’s all just context. Your careful instructions, the system prompt, the README, and a markdown blob some guy POSTed into your error tracker, same soup, same trust level, no little badges saying which parts came from you.
We spent two years calling prompt injection a chatbot party trick, the thing where you talk Bing into saying it loves you. It quietly stopped being a party trick the second we handed the model a shell and walked out of the room.

I built the exact pipe, so
I’m not writing this from a high horse, to be clear. I had Claude Code on auto-approve for tool calls because clicking allow forty times a session made me want to launch my laptop across the room, and go fix the Sentry issues unattended only works if it’s, you know, unattended. The whole value of the thing was that I wasn’t watching.
That’s also the whole exposure. Same property. The leash being long is the point and it’s the problem, and I genuinely don’t think you get one without the other. Still chewing on that part.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So what did I actually change. Killed the Friday ritual first. The agent doesn’t get to act on Sentry on its own anymore, it can read and summarize and propose, but nothing runs unless I’m looking at the command.
Rotated every token that box had reach into, because I had no clean way to know, and switched to scoped, short-lived ones wherever the service let me. Moved the riskier agent runs into a throwaway container, so the day one of these does get popped, and let’s be real, eventually one gets popped, it gets a sandbox instead of my SSH keys.
None of that is a fix. It’s friction, and I know it’s friction. The honest version is that the convenient setup and the safe setup are pulling in opposite directions, and I quietly picked convenient for six months without thinking about it once.
The bit I can’t shake is how boring the front door is. Not a kernel zero-day. Not some exotic ten-stage chain.
A bug report. The single most normal object in my entire job, the thing I’ve filed ten thousand of without a second thought, and it turns out it’s a way to type commands into my computer through a robot I asked to be helpful.
I still have the MCP servers installed. I’ll probably switch half of them back on next week when the fear wears off, because it always wears off, and because the workflow really was good.
I just won’t be at standup when it runs.
Anyway Go open devtools on your own site and find your DSN. Bet you it takes under ten seconds.
